content:
# Test Scope Dependencies

The `spring-boot-starter-test` starter (in the `test` `scope`) contains the following provided libraries:

* JUnit: The de-facto standard for unit testing Java applications.
* Spring Test & Spring Boot Test: Utilities and integration test support for Spring Boot applications.
* AssertJ: A fluent assertion library.
* Hamcrest: A library of matcher objects (also known as constraints or predicates).
* Mockito: A Java mocking framework.
* JSONassert: An assertion library for JSON.
* JsonPath: XPath for JSON.
* Awaitility: A library for testing asynchronous systems.

We generally find these common libraries to be useful when writing tests.
If these libraries do not suit your needs, you can add additional test dependencies of your own.
questions: Given a single development-only dependency bundle that supplies unit and integration harnesses, fluent assertion helpers, matcher utilities, dependency-simulation tools, structured-data assertion and query utilities, and asynchronous-testing aids—and noting that additional helpers can be added if required—what constraint or set of runtime hazards motivates keeping such a bundle out of deployed artifacts, and what concrete problems would arise if those components remained on the production execution path?

Given that the framework’s provided data-access helper can be replaced by a user-supplied implementation, that the auto-configuration layer locates DAO interfaces and domain types via package scanning unless overridden, and that the DAO-style interfaces in this integration cannot rely on inferring queries from method names, what inherent limitation of the wide‑column NoSQL support compels developers to supply explicit, annotated query definitions for finder operations instead of using convention-based query derivation?

```
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.SpringBootApplication
import org.springframework.boot.runApplication
import org.springframework.web.service.registry.ImportHttpServices

@SpringBootApplication
@ImportHttpServices(basePackages = ["com.example.myclients"])
class MyApplication

fun main(args: Array<String>) {
	runApplication<MyApplication>(*args)
}
```

### Service Client Groups

Hard-coding absolute URLs in `@HttpExchange` annotations is often not ideal in production applications.
Instead, you will typically want to give the HTTP Service client a logical name in your code, and then lookup a URL from a property based on that name.

HTTP Service clients allow you to do this by registering them into named groups.
An HTTP Service group is a collection of HTTP Service interfaces that all share common features.

For example, we may want to define an “echo” group to use for HTTP Service clients that call `https://echo.zuplo.io`.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
questions: Given that the application bootstrap explicitly brings in a package of remote-API client interfaces, what operational constraints and maintenance risks lead architects to prefer organizing those client types into named collections and resolving their base addresses from configuration rather than embedding literal addresses at each call site, and how does registering the client package during startup help mitigate those issues by enabling shared settings and centralized control?

content:
# Scripting

Redis versions 2.6 and higher provide support for running Lua scripts through the eval and evalsha commands. Spring Data Redis provides a high-level abstraction for running scripts that handles serialization and automatically uses the Redis script cache.

Scripts can be run by calling the `execute` methods of `RedisTemplate` and `ReactiveRedisTemplate`. Both use a configurable `ScriptExecutor` (or `ReactiveScriptExecutor`) to run the provided script. By default, the `ScriptExecutor` (or `ReactiveScriptExecutor`) takes care of serializing the provided keys and arguments and deserializing the script result. This is done through the key and value serializers of the template. There is an additional overload that lets you pass custom serializers for the script arguments and the result.
questions: Given an in‑memory key‑value engine that permits server‑side embedded scripts via two execution commands and a client library that exposes execute operations through both blocking and non‑blocking template wrappers, where the default execution component automatically serializes inputs and deserializes results using the template’s serializers and transparently uses a server‑side script caching mechanism, what interoperability or correctness limitation can arise at runtime that would justify providing an execute overload accepting user‑supplied serializers?

content:
#### Version-independent Settings

In addition to the API endpoint and token, you can also change the interval at which metrics are sent to Dynatrace.
The default export interval is `60s`.
The following example sets the export interval to 30 seconds:

* Properties
* YAML

```
management.dynatrace.metrics.export.step=30s
```

```
management:
  dynatrace:
    metrics:
      export:
        step: "30s"
```

You can find more information on how to set up the Dynatrace exporter for Micrometer in the Micrometer documentation and the Dynatrace documentation.

### Elastic

By default, metrics are exported to Elastic running on your local machine.
You can provide the location of the Elastic server to use by using the following property:

* Properties
* YAML

```
management.elastic.metrics.export.host=https://elastic.example.com:8086
```

```
management:
  elastic:
    metrics:
      export:
        host: "https://elastic.example.com:8086"
```

### Ganglia
questions: What operational trade-offs or constraints would motivate reducing the default telemetry emission cadence to a more frequent interval, and how would you specify the address of an external time-series backend in both flat key/value and nested configuration syntaxes to redirect the application's telemetry away from the local machine?

Given a platform that routes annotated background work to a designated runtime, why would an engineer choose to expose a general-purpose concurrent worker pool through a configuration-level adapter (or declare it the default candidate) rather than simply registering it under the runtime’s conventional identifier — what specific selection, lifecycle, or reuse conflicts does that pattern prevent and how do those constraints drive the architectural decision?

content:
|  | You need to ensure that names defined under `spring.pulsar.client.authentication.param.*` exactly match those expected by your auth plugin (which is typically camel cased). Spring Boot will not attempt any kind of relaxed binding for these entries.  For example, if you want to configure the issuer url for the `AuthenticationOAuth2` auth plugin you must use `spring.pulsar.client.authentication.param.issuerUrl`. If you use other forms, such as `issuerurl` or `issuer-url`, the setting will not be applied to the plugin.  This lack of relaxed binding also makes using environment variables for authentication parameters problematic because the case sensitivity is lost during translation. If you use environment variables for the parameters then you will need to follow these steps in the Spring for Apache Pulsar reference documentation for it to work properly. |

### SSL

By default, Pulsar clients communicate with Pulsar services in plain text.
questions: Given a messaging client whose credential keys must match the credential handler’s expected identifiers exactly (including exact letter-casing), and where supplying those keys via process-level key/value injection can alter their casing while the client also defaults to unencrypted transport, what combined configuration and security constraints would justify storing authentication parameters in a framework-managed, case-preserving configuration source and enabling transport encryption rather than relying on runtime key injection?

| `management.metrics.enable` | Whether to accept meters with certain IDs. Meters that are not accepted are filtered from the `MeterRegistry`. |
| `management.metrics.distribution.percentiles-histogram` | Whether to publish a histogram suitable for computing aggregable (across dimension) percentile approximations. |
| `management.metrics.distribution.minimum-expected-value`, `management.metrics.distribution.maximum-expected-value` | Publish fewer histogram buckets by clamping the range of expected values. |
| `management.metrics.distribution.percentiles` | Publish percentile values computed in your application |
| `management.metrics.distribution.expiry`, `management.metrics.distribution.buffer-length` | Give greater weight to recent samples by accumulating them in ring buffers which rotate after a configurable expiry, with a configurable buffer length. |
| `management.metrics.distribution.slo` | Publish a cumulative histogram with buckets defined by your service-level objectives. |
questions: Given a telemetry pipeline that first culls which instrument sources are admitted into the central store and that can either surface locally-computed rank-based summaries or publish cumulative frequency summaries aligned to explicit performance targets, what operational constraints or trade-offs would lead architects to clamp the expected numeric span and favor short-lived rotating sample buffers—thereby weighting recent observations more—rather than exporting full-resolution per-observation distributions?

content:
## Configuring Management-specific SSL

When configured to use a custom port, you can also configure the management server with its own SSL by using the various `management.server.ssl.*` properties.
For example, doing so lets a management server be available over HTTP while the main application uses HTTPS, as the following property settings show:

* Properties
* YAML

```
server.port=8443
server.ssl.enabled=true
server.ssl.key-store=classpath:store.jks
server.ssl.key-password=secret
management.server.port=8080
management.server.ssl.enabled=false
```

```
server:
  port: 8443
  ssl:
    enabled: true
    key-store: "classpath:store.jks"
    key-password: "secret"
management:
  server:
    port: 8080
    ssl:
      enabled: false
```

Alternatively, both the main server and the management server can use SSL but with different key stores, as follows:

* Properties
* YAML

```
server.port=8443
server.ssl.enabled=true
server.ssl.key-store=classpath:main.jks
server.ssl.key-password=secret
management.server.port=8080
management.server.ssl.enabled=true
management.server.ssl.key-store=classpath:management.jks
management.server.ssl.key-password=secret
questions: Given a deployment where the primary application and a separate administrative endpoint are exposed on different network ports—the former mandating encrypted connections backed by one local credential repository while the latter may be left unencrypted or provisioned with an independent credential store—what operational, security, or compatibility constraints would compel architects to segregate encryption policies and key material between these two listeners instead of consolidating them under a single certificate and port?

content:
Since Spring Boot relies entirely on the IDE to compile and copy files into the location from where Spring Boot can read them, you might find that there are times when certain changes are not reflected when devtools restarts the application.
If you observe such problems constantly, try increasing the `spring.devtools.restart.poll-interval` and `spring.devtools.restart.quiet-period` parameters to the values that fit your development environment:

* Properties
* YAML

```
spring.devtools.restart.poll-interval=2s
spring.devtools.restart.quiet-period=1s
```

```
spring:
  devtools:
    restart:
      poll-interval: "2s"
      quiet-period: "1s"
```

The monitored classpath directories are now polled every 2 seconds for changes, and a 1 second quiet period is maintained to make sure there are no additional class changes.
questions: Given that compilation and placement of build artifacts are delegated to the developer’s editor rather than the application process, which timing-related race condition between the filesystem-change detector and the restart sequencing motivates lengthening both the periodic scan cadence and the short stabilization delay so that reinitializations consistently observe fully written class files?

content:
## Using the Maven Plugin

The Spring Boot Maven plugin includes a `run` goal that can be used to quickly compile and run your application.
Applications run in an exploded form, as they do in your IDE.
The following example shows a typical Maven command to run a Spring Boot application:

```
$ mvn spring-boot:run
```

You might also want to use the `MAVEN_OPTS` operating system environment variable, as shown in the following example:

```
$ export MAVEN_OPTS=-Xmx1024m
```
questions: When a build-tool’s quick-execute goal compiles and launches an application directly from an unpacked project layout—so it runs in the same loose file/classpath arrangement used by IDEs—and the documentation recommends exporting a process-level options variable to increase the JVM heap for that run, what combination of packaging overhead, classpath resolution behavior, or process configuration limitations most plausibly explains why the implementers chose unpacked execution and rely on an external environment variable for memory tuning rather than embedding those settings into the tool’s normal packaging or configuration flow?

Given a configuration-binding system that instantiates a parent object together with a nested child via the parent’s instantiation parameters, why must an explicit empty-default marker be placed on the child parameter to cause a concrete child instance to be produced when no configuration entries exist, and how does the framework’s conversion of that marker’s textual value into the child’s target type interact with the language variant that enforces non-nullable parameters—thereby requiring those child parameters to be declared nullable for this approach to work?

content:
## Disabling Extended Cloud Foundry Actuator Support

If you want to fully disable the `/cloudfoundryapplication` endpoints, you can add the following setting to your `application.properties` file:

* Properties
* YAML

```
management.cloudfoundry.enabled=false
```

```
management:
  cloudfoundry:
    enabled: false
```
questions: To ensure a platform-targeted management route is completely disabled, which boolean configuration entry must be set to false — represented both as a flat key/value in a properties file and as a nested field in a hierarchical configuration document — and in which configuration locations should it be applied?

content:
```

Likewise, YAML lists also need to be flattened.
They are represented as property keys with `[index]` dereferencers.
For example, consider the following YAML:

```
 my:
  servers:
  - "dev.example.com"
  - "another.example.com"
```

The preceding example would be transformed into these properties:

```
my.servers[0]=dev.example.com
my.servers[1]=another.example.com
```

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | Properties that use the `[index]` notation can be bound to Java `List` or `Set` objects using Spring Boot’s `Binder` class. For more details see the Type-safe Configuration Properties section below. |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | YAML files cannot be loaded by using the `@PropertySource` or `@TestPropertySource` annotations. So, in the case that you need to load values that way, you need to use a properties file. |

### Directly Loading YAML

Spring Framework provides two convenient classes that can be used to load YAML documents.
questions: Because a nested, indentation-based configuration file can be flattened into dotted paths with bracketed numeric indices to represent sequences, what limitation of the annotation-driven resource-inclusion mechanism forces practitioners to first turn that hierarchical source into a simple key=value style before framework-managed collection members can be populated, and how do the framework’s dedicated document-parsing utilities eliminate the need for that intermediate conversion?

content:
| `List<T>` | A `List`. |
| `Optional<T>` | A Java 8 or Guava `Optional`. Expects the query method to return one result at most. If no result is found, `Optional.empty()` or `Optional.absent()` is returned. More than one result triggers an `IncorrectResultSizeDataAccessException`. |
| `Option<T>` | Either a Scala or Vavr `Option` type. Semantically the same behavior as Java 8’s `Optional`, described earlier. |
| `Stream<T>` | A Java 8 `Stream`. |
| `Streamable<T>` | A convenience extension of `Iterable` that directly exposes methods to stream, map and filter results, concatenate them etc. |
| Types that implement `Streamable` and take a `Streamable` constructor or factory method argument | Types that expose a constructor or `….of(…)`/`….valueOf(…)` factory method taking a `Streamable` as argument. See Returning Custom Streamable Wrapper Types for details. |
| Vavr `Seq`, `List`, `Map`, `Set` | Vavr collection types. See Support for Vavr Collections for details. |
questions: Given a data-access API that exposes both a zero-or-one element holder (which fails when more than one match is returned) and separate lazy pipeline-style result variants with adapters to produce various collection wrappers, what constraint in result multiplicity and caller-side expectations forces designers to supply distinct singular-result operations instead of consolidating all queries into sequence-returning methods?

Given a type bound under a namespaced key that itself contains non-alphanumeric separators and exposing a single string-valued accessor, what backward-compatibility or environment-compatibility concern motivates the system that maps externalized settings to treat punctuation- and case-variant external key forms as equivalent when resolving that accessor?

content:
## Fluent API

`QueryByExampleExecutor` defines fluent query methods for flexible execution of queries based on `Example` instances through `findBy(Example<S> example, Function<FluentQuery.FetchableFluentQuery<S>, R> queryFunction)`.

As with other methods, it executes a query derived from a `Example`.
However, the query function allows you to take control over aspects of query execution that you cannot dynamically control otherwise.
You do so by invoking the various intermediate and terminal methods of `FetchableFluentQuery`.

**Intermediate methods**

* `sortBy`: Apply an ordering for your result.
  Repeated method calls append each `Sort` (note that `page(Pageable)` using a sorted `Pageable` overrides any previous sort order).
* `limit`: Limit the result count.
* `as`: Specify the type to be read or projected to.
* `project`: Limit the queries properties.

**Terminal methods**
questions: Given an API that derives searches from a template object but also returns a customizable execution handle exposing chainable operations for cumulative ordering, result truncation, runtime type conversion and property restriction—while permitting a paged request to supersede any previously specified ordering—what intrinsic shortcomings of the template-based approach and what conflict-resolution needs justify exposing both appendable modifiers and a finalizing execution stage, and in what ways do those capabilities together address those shortcomings?

If two test-oriented documentation utilities operate against the same underlying simulated HTTP test harness—so any tweak to that harness affects both—and the declarative auto-configuration options cannot express a different rendering style for generated snippets, what is the justification for adding a test-scoped configuration component that programmatically alters the documentation generator’s snippet rendering format, and how does the shared harness constrain where and how that customization must be applied?

The path can be customized with the `spring.webflux.webjars-path-pattern` property.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | Spring WebFlux applications do not strictly depend on the servlet API, so they cannot be deployed as war files and do not use the `src/main/webapp` directory. |

### Welcome Page

Spring Boot supports both static and templated welcome pages.
It first looks for an `index.html` file in the configured static content locations.
If one is not found, it then looks for an `index` template.
If either is found, it is automatically used as the welcome page of the application.

This only acts as a fallback for actual index routes defined by the application.
The ordering is defined by the order of `HandlerMapping` beans which is by default the following:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| `org.springframework.web.reactive.function.server.support.RouterFunctionMapping` | Endpoints declared with `RouterFunction` beans |
questions: Because this runtime avoids dependence on the conventional container request API—preventing it from being packaged as a traditional deployable archive or from using the standard webapp source folder—and because the platform locates an application’s default entry by first scanning configured file-based assets and only then checking for a renderable view, how does the default ordering that evaluates programmatically registered routing components before those file-or-view checks justify treating discovered files or renderable views as fallbacks rather than as primary entry points that would override explicitly declared endpoints?

content:
* Bootstrap executor for background initialization of beans in the `ApplicationContext`.

While this approach works in most scenarios, Spring Boot allows you to override the auto-configured `AsyncTaskExecutor`.
By default, when a custom `Executor` bean is registered, the auto-configured `AsyncTaskExecutor` backs off, and the custom `Executor` is used for regular task execution (via `@EnableAsync`).

However, Spring MVC, Spring WebFlux, and Spring GraphQL all require a bean named `applicationTaskExecutor`.
For Spring MVC and Spring WebFlux, this bean must be of type `AsyncTaskExecutor`, whereas Spring GraphQL does not enforce this type requirement.

Spring WebSocket and JPA will use `AsyncTaskExecutor` if either a single bean of this type is available or a bean named `applicationTaskExecutor` is defined.

Finally, the boostrap executor of the `ApplicationContext` uses a bean named `applicationTaskExecutor` unless a bean named `bootstrapExecutor` is defined.
questions: What interoperability constraint compels an application to register a separately named startup task runner rather than merely replacing the platform’s default background-runner when a user-provided implementation causes the default to withdraw, considering that different subsystems resolve their runtime runner either by a specific component name or by conformance to a certain execution interface (and that one subsystem only requires the name without enforcing the interface)?

content:
The `PulsarTemplate` relies on a `PulsarProducerFactory` to create the underlying Pulsar producer.
Spring Boot auto-configuration also provides this producer factory, which by default, caches the producers that it creates.
You can configure the producer factory and cache settings by specifying any of the `spring.pulsar.producer.*` and `spring.pulsar.producer.cache.*` prefixed application properties.

If you need more control over the producer factory configuration, consider registering one or more `ProducerBuilderCustomizer` beans.
These customizers are applied to all created producers.
You can also pass in a `ProducerBuilderCustomizer` when sending a message to only affect the current producer.

If you need more control over the message being sent, you can pass in a `TypedMessageBuilderCustomizer` when sending a message.
questions: Given that the messaging template depends on an auto-provisioned creation component which, by default, returns reused sending instances, while the platform allows both globally-registered construction-time modifier beans (affecting all created senders) and ad-hoc message-level modifier arguments (intended to alter only the current send), what runtime phenomenon could cause an ad-hoc message-level tweak to be ignored, and how should one adjust either the reuse-control application settings or the location of the construction-time modifiers to guarantee that per-message adjustments take effect?

content:
# Efficient Container Images

It is easily possible to package a Spring Boot uber jar as a Docker image.
However, there are various downsides to copying and running the uber jar as-is in the Docker image.
There’s always a certain amount of overhead when running an uber jar without unpacking it, and in a containerized environment this can be noticeable.
The other issue is that putting your application’s code and all its dependencies in one layer in the Docker image is not optimal.
Since you probably recompile your code more often than you upgrade the version of Spring Boot you use, it’s often better to separate things a bit more.
If you put jar files in the layer before your application classes, Docker often only needs to change the very bottom layer and can pick others up from its cache.
questions: Which operational constraints and performance trade-offs—for example the additional cost of executing a single packaged application archive without expanding it in an isolated runtime and the reduced opportunity to reuse unchanged dependency binaries when application code is recompiled frequently—motivate splitting dependency artifacts into earlier filesystem strata while keeping compiled application classes separate, instead of bundling everything together into one monolithic archive?

Given an application bootstrap class that auto-registers interface-based HTTP clients discovered in a particular code namespace under a named client identifier, and an externalized configuration that maps that identifier to a single remote endpoint, what operational or deployment constraint would most likely motivate centralizing those clients at startup and exposing their target address via configuration rather than wiring endpoints inline or resolving them per invocation?

Given an application-level HTTP helper that is configured with a bespoke low-level requester which, by default, channels outbound requests through an intermediary agent but intentionally sends calls for one specific internal destination directly, what mix of operational, testing, and security constraints would motivate both injecting that bespoke requester into the helper’s transport layer and excluding that internal endpoint from the intermediary path?

content:
|  | `optional:` prefixes are not considered when determining if a location is fixed or import relative. |

As an example, say we have a `/demo` directory containing our `application.jar` file.
We might add a `/demo/application.properties` file with the following content:

```
spring.config.import=optional:core/core.properties
```

This is an import relative location and so will attempt to load the file `/demo/core/core.properties` if it exists.

If `/demo/core/core.properties` has the following content:

```
spring.config.import=optional:extra/extra.properties
```

It will attempt to load `/demo/core/extra/extra.properties`.
The `optional:extra/extra.properties` is relative to `/demo/core/core.properties` so the full directory is `/demo/core/` + `extra/extra.properties`.

#### Property Ordering

The order an import is defined inside a single document within the properties/yaml file does not matter.
For instance, the two examples below produce the same result:

* Properties
* YAML

```
spring.config.import=my.properties
my.property=value
questions: Given a configuration file located in one directory that requests the system to pull in another configuration fragment by a relative path, and that second fragment itself names a further fragment with a permissive marker, explain why the resolver ends up looking for the third fragment under the second fragment’s directory rather than under the original file’s directory, and how the permissive marker affects error handling if that nested fragment is absent, considering that the order of such pull-in directives inside a single document does not change resolution behavior.

content:
Dynatrace auto-configuration is available for hosts that are monitored by the OneAgent or by the Dynatrace Operator for Kubernetes.

**Local OneAgent:** If a OneAgent is running on the host, metrics are automatically exported to the local OneAgent ingest endpoint.
The ingest endpoint forwards the metrics to the Dynatrace backend.

**Dynatrace Kubernetes Operator:** When running in Kubernetes with the Dynatrace Operator installed, the registry will automatically pick up your endpoint URI and API token from the operator instead.

This is the default behavior and requires no special setup beyond a dependency on `io.micrometer:micrometer-registry-dynatrace`.

##### Manual Configuration

If no auto-configuration is available, the endpoint of the Metrics v2 API and an API token are required.
The API token must have the “Ingest metrics” (`metrics.ingest`) permission set.
We recommend limiting the scope of the token to this one permission.
questions: Given that when a host runs a local collector or when a cluster-level monitoring controller is present the client-side registry can automatically learn both where to send time-series and which secret to use with only the library dependency, what operational constraints and security risks justify allowing a fallback manual configuration that requires explicitly providing the endpoint plus a tightly-scoped submission credential rather than permitting broader or centrally preconfigured credentials?

| `spring-boot-starter-data-mongodb` | Starter for using MongoDB document-oriented database and Spring Data MongoDB |
| `spring-boot-starter-data-mongodb-reactive` | Starter for using MongoDB document-oriented database and Spring Data MongoDB Reactive |
| `spring-boot-starter-data-mongodb-reactive-test` | Starter for using MongoDB document-oriented database and Spring Data MongoDB Reactive |
| `spring-boot-starter-data-mongodb-test` | Starter for testing MongoDB document-oriented database and Spring Data MongoDB |
| `spring-boot-starter-data-neo4j` | Starter for using Neo4j graph database and Spring Data Neo4j |
| `spring-boot-starter-data-neo4j-test` | Starter for testing Neo4j graph database and Spring Data Neo4j |
| `spring-boot-starter-data-r2dbc` | Starter for using Spring Data R2DBC |
| `spring-boot-starter-data-r2dbc-test` | Starter for testing Spring Data R2DBC |
questions: Given an ecosystem that ships separate modules for each persistence paradigm (a document-oriented store, a graph store, and an asynchronous stream-oriented relational connector), and that additionally provides distinct artifacts tailored for the non-blocking programming model and for test-time consumption, what compatibility or lifecycle constraints in the platform and client libraries would justify producing these parallel variants instead of a single consolidated package?

content:
## Configuring System Environment Properties

Spring Boot supports setting a prefix for environment properties.
This is useful if the system environment is shared by multiple Spring Boot applications with different configuration requirements.
The prefix for system environment properties can be set directly on `SpringApplication` by calling the `setEnvironmentPrefix(…​)` method before the application is run.

For example, if you set the prefix to `input`, a property such as `remote.timeout` will be resolved as `INPUT_REMOTE_TIMEOUT` in the system environment.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | The prefix *only* applies to system environment properties. The example above would continue to use `remote.timeout` when reading properties from other sources. |
questions: Given a framework in which the application launcher can attach a leading label to keys coming from the process-level key/value store—an attachment that must be made before startup and that deliberately leaves keys from other configuration sources unchanged—what operational or resolution constraints would justify restricting that labeling to only the process-level source and requiring it to be set prior to the application run?

content:
```

```
import org.springframework.boot.context.properties.ConfigurationProperties

@ConfigurationProperties("my.props")
class MyMapsProperties {

	val values: Map<String, String> = HashMap()

}
```

When setting `MY_PROPS_VALUES_KEY=value`, the `values` `Map` contains a `{"key"="value"}` entry.

Only the environment variable **name** is lower-cased, not the value.
When setting `MY_PROPS_VALUES_KEY=VALUE`, the `values` `Map` contains a `{"key"="VALUE"}` entry.

#### Caching

Relaxed binding uses a cache to improve performance. By default, this caching is only applied to immutable property sources.
To customize this behavior, for example to enable caching for mutable property sources, use `ConfigurationPropertyCaching`.

### Merging Complex Types

When lists are configured in more than one place, overriding works by replacing the entire list.

For example, assume a `MyPojo` object with `name` and `description` attributes that are `null` by default.
questions: Given a mapping component that normalizes only external identifier names into a canonical lowercase form while preserving the original casing of their associated data, that by default retains resolved entries in a temporary lookup store only for sources that cannot be changed, and that treats overrides of ordered collections as full replacements rather than element-wise merges, what set of consistency, collision, and staleness concerns most plausibly motivated these three interrelated design trade-offs?

content:
Spring Boot provides auto-configuration for Spring WebFlux that works well with most applications.

The auto-configuration adds the following features on top of Spring’s defaults:

* Configuring codecs for `HttpMessageReader` and `HttpMessageWriter` instances (described later in this document).
* Support for serving static resources, including support for WebJars (described later in this document).

If you want to keep Spring Boot WebFlux features and you want to add additional WebFlux configuration, you can add your own `@Configuration` class of type `WebFluxConfigurer` but **without** `@EnableWebFlux`.

If you want to add additional customization to the auto-configured `HttpHandler`, you can define beans of type `WebHttpHandlerBuilderCustomizer` and use them to modify the `WebHttpHandlerBuilder`.

If you want to take complete control of Spring WebFlux, you can add your own `@Configuration` annotated with `@EnableWebFlux`.

### Spring WebFlux Conversion Service
questions: Given an auto-provisioned reactive web layer that already registers server-side message (de)serializers and static asset handling, why must one avoid flipping the switch that replaces the auto-wired stack with a fully manual reactive-web setup when only augmenting configuration, and which bean-based customization entry point should be used to adjust the constructed request-handling pipeline without losing the framework’s automatic provisions?

content:
## Ant

It is possible to build a Spring Boot project using Apache Ant+Ivy.
The `spring-boot-antlib` “AntLib” module is also available to help Ant create executable jars.

To declare dependencies, a typical `ivy.xml` file looks something like the following example:

```
<ivy-module version="2.0">
	<info organisation="org.springframework.boot" module="spring-boot-sample-ant" />
	<configurations>
		<conf name="compile" description="everything needed to compile this module" />
		<conf name="runtime" extends="compile" description="everything needed to run this module" />
	</configurations>
	<dependencies>
		<dependency org="org.springframework.boot" name="spring-boot-starter"
			rev="${spring-boot.version}" conf="compile" />
	</dependencies>
</ivy-module>
```

A typical `build.xml` looks like the following example:

```
<project
	xmlns:ivy="antlib:org.apache.ivy.ant"
	xmlns:spring-boot="antlib:org.springframework.boot.ant"
	name="myapp" default="build">

	<property name="spring-boot.version" value="4.0.3" />

	<target name="resolve" description="--> retrieve dependencies with ivy">
		<ivy:retrieve pattern="lib/[conf]/[artifact]-[type]-[revision].[ext]" />
	</target>

	<target name="classpaths" depends="resolve">
		<path id="compile.classpath">
			<fileset dir="lib/compile" includes="*.jar" />
		</path>
	</target>

	<target name="init" depends="classpaths">
		<mkdir dir="build/classes" />
	</target>

	<target name="compile" depends="init" description="compile">
		<javac srcdir="src/main/java" destdir="build/classes" classpathref="compile.classpath" />
	</target>

	<target name="build" depends="compile">
		<spring-boot:exejar destfile="build/myapp.jar" classes="build/classes">
			<spring-boot:lib>
				<fileset dir="lib/runtime" />
			</spring-boot:lib>
		</spring-boot:exejar>
	</target>
</project>
questions: Given a workflow where a dependency descriptor distinguishes build-time versus execution-time scopes, a resolver lays out resolved libraries into scope-labeled folders, the compiler constructs its classpath from the build-time folder, and the packaging helper creates a single runnable archive by including only the execution-time folder alongside compiled classes, what inherent constraint of placing artifacts into separate scope directories and referencing them only for compilation prevents the packager from automatically bundling the build-time libraries into the final artifact and thus forces the explicit fetch-then-reference sequence?

content:
By default, Spring Boot’s Docker Compose support is disabled when running tests.

To enable Docker Compose support in tests, set `spring.docker.compose.skip.in-tests` to `false`.

When using Gradle, you also need to change the configuration of the `spring-boot-docker-compose` dependency from `developmentOnly` to `testAndDevelopmentOnly`:

Gradle

```
dependencies {
	testAndDevelopmentOnly("org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-docker-compose")
}
```
questions: When a project’s integration with local container orchestration is inactive during automated test runs, why is it necessary both to clear the in-test skip toggle to permit activation and to widen the build tool’s dependency scope from development-only to one that participates in tests, and what aspect of the test/build lifecycle makes both steps required rather than just one?

content:
|  | To use a reserved keyword in the name of a property, such as `my.service.import`, use the `@Name` annotation on the property’s field. |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | The properties that map to `@ConfigurationProperties` classes available in Spring Boot, which are configured through properties files, YAML files, environment variables, and other mechanisms, are public API but the accessors (getters/setters) of the class itself are not meant to be used directly. |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
questions: In a system where externally supplied configuration entries mapped onto plain data-holder classes are treated as the supported public contract (and can come from multiple external mechanisms), while the classes’ internal read/write routines are discouraged for direct use, and where a metadata marker is provided to represent an entry name that would otherwise conflict with language-level identifiers, what stability or compatibility concern and naming-collision risk are being mitigated by promoting the external binding surface as the public API, discouraging direct invocation of object-level access routines, and introducing the metadata-based escape?

Given that the framework provides a ready-made publish helper which user components receive via constructor parameters in both illustrated JVM language variants and then invoke from instance methods to emit plain-text payloads to named channels, what implicit assumption about that helper’s lifecycle scope and concurrency safety justifies the framework auto-supplying it rather than requiring callers to create one on each use?

content:
|  | Each slice restricts component scan to appropriate components and loads a very restricted set of auto-configuration classes. If you need to exclude one of them, most `@…​Test` annotations provide an `excludeAutoConfiguration` attribute. Alternatively, you can use `@ImportAutoConfiguration#exclude`. |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | Including multiple “slices” by using several `@…​Test` annotations in one test is not supported. If you need multiple “slices”, pick one of the `@…​Test` annotations and include the `@AutoConfigure…​` annotations of the other “slices” by hand. |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | It is also possible to use the `@AutoConfigure…​` annotations with the standard `@SpringBootTest` annotation. You can use this combination if you are not interested in “slicing” your application but you want some of the auto-configured test beans. |
questions: What architectural constraints and dependency-resolution risks justify designing specialized test markers to severely limit component discovery and activate only a minimal subset of framework startup recipes—thereby disallowing multiple such markers in the same case (forcing one to choose one and manually add others’ startup recipes), offering an explicit opt-out for particular startup recipes, and recommending the fallback of running a full integration-style test while selectively enabling specific startup recipes when multiple focused behaviors are required?

content:
# Aspect-Oriented Programming

Spring Boot provides auto-configuration for aspect-oriented programming (AOP).
You can learn more about AOP with Spring in the Spring Framework reference documentation.

By default, Spring Boot’s auto-configuration configures Spring AOP to use CGLib proxies.
To use JDK proxies instead, set `spring.aop.proxy-target-class` to `false`.

If AspectJ is on the classpath, Spring Boot’s auto-configuration will automatically enable AspectJ auto proxy such that `@EnableAspectJAutoProxy` is not required.
questions: Given that the system prefers a subclassing-based interposition strategy by default yet exposes a configuration toggle to switch to an interface-delegation approach, and that the runtime presence of a bytecode-level aspect weaver causes automatic activation without needing an explicit enablement marker, what runtime compatibility constraints and proxying limitations justify the subclassing default and the existence of the toggle, and how does the weaver’s presence obviate the need for an explicit activation hint?

content:
|  | A wildcard location must contain only one `*` and end with `*/` for search locations that are directories or `*/<filename>` for search locations that are files. Locations with wildcards are sorted alphabetically based on the absolute path of the file names. |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | Wildcard locations only work with external directories. You cannot use a wildcard in a `classpath:` location. |

### Profile Specific Files

As well as `application` property files, Spring Boot will also attempt to load profile-specific files using the naming convention `application-{profile}`.
For example, if your application activates a profile named `prod` and uses YAML files, then both `application.yaml` and `application-prod.yaml` will be considered.

Profile-specific properties are loaded from the same locations as standard `application.properties`, with profile-specific files always overriding the non-specific ones.
If several profiles are specified, a last-wins strategy applies.
questions: Given a system that searches the same set of candidate paths for both base and environment-tailored configuration documents—applying a precedence rule where later-discovered entries override earlier ones—and that requires any path pattern to contain exactly one path placeholder which must terminate with a trailing directory marker, what operational hazard or correctness constraint motivates enforcing that strict single-placeholder, directory-terminated pattern when resolving environment-dependent configuration?

content:
|  | If you need to register extra components, such as a `JacksonModule`, you can import additional configuration classes by using `@Import` on your test. |

Often, `@WebMvcTest` is limited to a single controller and is used in combination with `@MockitoBean` to provide mock implementations for required collaborators.

`@WebMvcTest` also auto-configures `MockMvc`.
Mock MVC offers a powerful way to quickly test MVC controllers without needing to start a full HTTP server.
If AssertJ is available, the AssertJ support provided by `MockMvcTester` is auto-configured as well.
If you’d like to use `RestTestClient` in your tests, annotate your test class with `@AutoConfigureRestTestClient`.
A `RestTestClient` that uses the Mock MVC infrastructure will then be auto-configured.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
questions: Given a focused layer test that automatically provides an in-process HTTP-like dispatcher and can wire in a fluent assertion integration when available, what constraints and interoperability issues drive practitioners to restrict the test to a single request-handling unit, supply substitute implementations for its dependent collaborators, and sometimes register extra data-binding/serialization modules at the test level when they choose to exercise those handlers via an alternative client that reuses the same in-process dispatcher?

content:
To add to the default tags, provide a `@Bean` that extends `DefaultServerRequestObservationConvention` from the `org.springframework.http.server.observation` package.
To replace the default tags, provide a `@Bean` that implements `ServerRequestObservationConvention`.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | In some cases, exceptions handled in web controllers are not recorded as request metrics tags. Applications can opt in and record exceptions by setting handled exceptions as request attributes. |

By default, all requests are handled.
To customize the filter, provide a `@Bean` that implements `FilterRegistrationBean<ServerHttpObservationFilter>`.

### Spring WebFlux Metrics

Auto-configuration enables the instrumentation of all requests handled by Spring WebFlux controllers and functional handlers.
By default, metrics are generated with the name, `http.server.requests`.
You can customize the name by setting the `management.observations.http.server.requests.name` property.
questions: If a web platform autonomously attaches a default set of telemetry labels to every incoming server call but omits handled error outcomes unless the application explicitly annotates the request, and if changing the label set or swapping the request observation filter requires supplying custom components via the application’s component registry, what practical constraints or trade-offs (for example: uncontrolled label proliferation, preserving backward compatibility, limiting runtime overhead, or avoiding inadvertent exposure of internal error details) likely drove the decision to make error-reporting opt‑in and customization available only through component registration?

content:
## OpenTelemetry Support

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | There are several ways to support OpenTelemetry in your application. You can use the OpenTelemetry Java Agent or the OpenTelemetry Spring Boot Starter, which are supported by the OTel community; the metrics and traces use the semantic conventions defined by OTel libraries. This documentation describes OpenTelemetry as officially supported by the Spring team, using Micrometer and the OTLP exporter; the metrics and traces use the semantic conventions described in the Spring projects documentation, such as Spring Framework. |

Spring Boot’s actuator module includes basic support for OpenTelemetry.

It provides a bean of type `OpenTelemetry`, and if there are beans of type `SdkTracerProvider`, `ContextPropagators`, `SdkLoggerProvider` or `SdkMeterProvider` in the application context, they automatically get registered.
Additionally, it provides a `Resource` bean.
questions: Given that the platform documents external runtime instrumentation options and an in-application metrics façade with a network exporter that all adhere to a common naming schema, what interoperability or deployment constraints motivate the runtime’s management component to expose a single observability interface and automatically wire any present telemetry SDK pieces plus a resource descriptor into the application context instead of leaving integration entirely to external instrumentation?

Given that locale-to-encoding pairs can be expressed declaratively but can also be applied by registering a runtime component that alters the embedded HTTP server (for example to change its network listening endpoint), what kind of deployment or runtime constraint would force engineers to prefer the programmatic server-modifying approach over relying on the equivalent declarative mappings used by legacy archive-based deployments?

content:
## Nullability in Kotlin-based Repositories

Kotlin has the definition of nullability constraints baked into the language.
Kotlin code compiles to bytecode, which does not express nullability constraints through method signatures but rather through compiled-in metadata.
Make sure to include the `kotlin-reflect` JAR in your project to enable introspection of Kotlin’s nullability constraints.
Spring Data repositories use the language mechanism to define those constraints to apply the same runtime checks, as follows:

Using nullability constraints on Kotlin repositories

```
interface UserRepository : Repository<User, String> {

  fun findByUsername(username: String): User     (1)

  fun findByFirstname(firstname: String?): User? (2)
}
```

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
questions: In a JVM-targeting language where source-level optionality is encoded in the language but not represented in runtime method descriptors, why must a runtime inspection helper JAR be present so that a persistence-oriented framework’s interface-based components can observe and enforce those optionality rules at execution time, and by what mechanism do the compiler-emitted descriptive annotations enable that enforcement despite the absence of explicit optionality in the runtime signatures?

Because the handler that returns related entries first performs a lookup by a numeric key, applies a transformation to the potential result, then forcibly extracts the outcome, which architectural weakness causes a missing primary record to trigger an unhandled runtime failure (leading to an internal-server response) rather than a not-found response, and why does the handler that removes a record by the same key exhibit different observable behavior when the key does not exist?

If a prototype-driven retrieval layer constructs queries solely from attributes that have backing lookup structures in the datastore, and the store combines those lookup entries into result key sets while insisting that supplied attributes be matched literally and be present, what indexing-configuration rationale explains why any prototype attribute without a corresponding lookup is automatically omitted to avoid producing accidentally over-constrained or empty result sets?

content:
Using the same field/column name for different values typically leads to corrupt data so you should annotate least one of the properties using an explicit field/column name.
3. Using `@AccessType(PROPERTY)` cannot be used as the super-property cannot be set.

### Kotlin Value Classes

Kotlin Value Classes are designed for a more expressive domain model to make underlying concepts explicit.
Spring Data can read and write types that define properties using Value Classes.

Consider the following domain model:

```
@JvmInline
value class EmailAddress(val theAddress: String)                                    (1)

data class Contact(val id: String, val name:String, val emailAddress: EmailAddress) (2)
```

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **1** | A simple value class with a non-nullable value type. |
| **2** | Data class defining a property using the `EmailAddress` value class. |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
questions: For a domain model that encapsulates string values in thin wrapper types and is persisted by a mapping framework, what internal constraint makes it necessary to explicitly name at least one storage attribute when two different properties would otherwise collapse to the same storage identifier (thus avoiding corruption), and why is switching the mapping to use method-centered access not a viable workaround when the framework’s global access strategy cannot be changed?

If an application supplies its own HTTP-client construction helper that overrides the framework‑provided instance, what limitation makes it necessary to funnel the framework’s modifier components through a distinct configurator, and why must that configurator be explicitly applied when building the custom helper to retain framework-registered adjustments such as altered connection and response wait durations?

content:
## Auto-configuration Packages

Auto-configuration packages are the packages that various auto-configured features look in by default when scanning for things such as entities and Spring Data repositories.
The `@EnableAutoConfiguration` annotation (either directly or through its presence on `@SpringBootApplication`) determines the default auto-configuration package.
Additional packages can be configured using the `@AutoConfigurationPackage` annotation.
questions: Given a framework where an application-level declarative marker determines the default namespace the runtime will scan for components such as persisted domain classes and data-access interfaces, what architectural drawbacks stem from deriving that discovery root from the marked class’s namespace, and how does introducing a distinct declarative marker that explicitly registers additional namespaces address those drawbacks?

content:
# Hash Mapping

Data can be stored by using various data structures within Redis. `JacksonJsonRedisSerializer` can convert objects in JSON format.
Ideally, JSON can be stored as a value by using plain keys.
You can achieve a more sophisticated mapping of structured objects by using Redis hashes.
Spring Data Redis offers various strategies for mapping data to hashes (depending on the use case):

* Direct mapping, by using `HashOperations` and a serializer
* Using Redis Repositories
* Using `HashMapper` and `HashOperations`
questions: Given a system where objects may be persisted either as a single serialized structured string under a plain key by a serializer or broken down into the datastore's native associative records via an object-to-map translator and map-operation APIs, what concrete operational shortcoming of the monolithic serialized-value approach forces architects to prefer the decomposed mapping when requirements demand efficient partial updates, selective attribute reads, safe concurrent modifications, and smoother interoperability with other clients?

content:
The application’s single required argument is the remote URL to which it connects.

For example, if you are using Eclipse or Spring Tools and you have a project named `my-app` that you have deployed to Cloud Foundry, you would do the following:

* Select `Run Configurations…​` from the `Run` menu.
* Create a new `Java Application` “launch configuration”.
* Browse for the `my-app` project.
* Use `RemoteSpringApplication` as the main class.
* Add `https://myapp.cfapps.io` to the `Program arguments` (or whatever your remote URL is).

A running remote client might resemble the following listing:

```
  .   ____          _                                              __ _ _

( ( )\___ | '_ | '_| | '_ \/ _` |        | _ \___ _ __  ___| |_ ___ \ \ \ \

  '  |____| .__|_| |_|_| |_\__, |        |_|_\___|_|_|_\___/\__\___|/ / / /
 =========|_|==============|___/===================================/_/_/_/

2026-02-19T12:22:43.904Z  INFO 129087 --- [           main] o.s.b.devtools.RemoteSpringApplication   : Starting RemoteSpringApplication v4.0.3 using Java 25.0.2 with PID 129087 (/Users/myuser/.m2/repository/org/springframework/boot/spring-boot-devtools/4.0.3/spring-boot-devtools-4.0.3.jar started by myuser in /opt/apps/)

2026-02-19T12:22:44.829Z  INFO 129087 --- [           main] o.s.b.devtools.RemoteSpringApplication   : Started RemoteSpringApplication in 2.157 seconds (process running for 2.975)
questions: Given an IDE-based launch workflow where a developer picks a workspace project and assigns an entry-point component, then invokes the tool with a single runtime parameter pointing at an external endpoint, and the resulting startup trace shows a fallback to one default runtime configuration while an automatic client-refresh daemon is spawned on a development socket, what deployment or developer-experience constraints most plausibly drove the decision to require only the remote address as the lone invocation argument?

content:
```

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | All property keys must be unique. As long as a property starts with `restart.include.` or `restart.exclude.` it is considered. |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | All `META-INF/spring-devtools.properties` from the classpath are loaded. You can package files inside your project, or in the libraries that the project consumes. System properties can not be used, only the properties file. |

### Known Limitations

Restart functionality does not work well with objects that are deserialized by using a standard `ObjectInputStream`.
If you need to deserialize data, you may need to use Spring’s `ConfigurableObjectInputStream` in combination with `Thread.currentThread().getContextClassLoader()`.

Unfortunately, several third-party libraries deserialize without considering the context classloader.
If you find such a problem, you need to request a fix with the original authors.
questions: Why does the development-time module-refresh mechanism deliberately load its configuration only from packaged resource entries on the runtime module search path instead of honoring externally provided runtime flags, and how does the requirement to rebuild serialized object graphs using a type-resolution routine that consults the current execution context both motivate that configuration-loading design and explain the breakage that occurs when dependent libraries reconstruct objects without using the context-aware resolver, necessitating fixes in those libraries?

content:
For example, with the appropriate Eclipse or Spring Tools plugin installed, you can press `ctrl-space` in the POM editor and type “spring-boot-starter” for a complete list.

As explained in the Creating Your Own Starter section, third party starters should not start with `spring-boot`, as it is reserved for official Spring Boot artifacts.
Rather, a third-party starter typically starts with the name of the project.
For example, a third-party starter project called `thirdpartyproject` would typically be named `thirdpartyproject-spring-boot-starter`.

The following application starters are provided by Spring Boot under the `org.springframework.boot` group:

Table 1. Spring Boot application starters

| Name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `spring-boot-starter` | Core starter, including auto-configuration support, logging and YAML |
| `spring-boot-starter-activemq` | Starter for using Apache ActiveMQ and JMS |
questions: Given that the build descriptor editor can auto-suggest complete module identifiers and the platform publishes official initialization packages—including a core package that supplies default wiring, logging and a human-readable settings format, and a separate package that binds a particular messaging broker—what kinds of namespace collisions or behavioral ambiguities would justify prohibiting third-party providers from using the official prefix and instead requiring them to use their own project identifier in package names?

content:
Classes used reflectively, especially annotations, can be loaded into the parent (fixed) classloader on startup before the application classes which use them, and this might lead to them not being detected by Spring in the application.

You can instruct Spring Boot to load parts of your project with a different classloader by creating a `META-INF/spring-devtools.properties` file.
The `spring-devtools.properties` file can contain properties prefixed with `restart.exclude` and `restart.include`.
The `include` elements are items that should be pulled up into the “restart” classloader, and the `exclude` elements are items that should be pushed down into the “base” classloader.
The value of the property is a regex pattern that is applied to the classpath passed to the JVM on startup.
Here is an example where some local class files are excluded and some extra libraries are included in the restart class loader:

```
restart:
  exclude:
    companycommonlibs: "/mycorp-common-[\\w\\d-\\.]/(build|bin|out|target)/"
  include:
    projectcommon: "/mycorp-myproj-[\\w\\d-\\.]+\\.jar"
questions: What specific interoperability or component-discovery failure—where metadata accessed reflectively by the framework is pulled into a higher-level loading context before the application’s own classes and thus becomes invisible to the application’s automatic discovery—would motivate adding a small, startup-read configuration that selectively reassigns particular compiled outputs and external libraries between the shared and an isolated loading context using location-matching rules?

content:
## Connecting to Pulsar Administration

Spring for Apache Pulsar’s `PulsarAdministration` client is also auto-configured.

By default, the application tries to connect to a local Pulsar instance at `http://localhost:8080`.
This can be adjusted by setting the `spring.pulsar.admin.service-url` property to a different value in the form `(http|https)://<host>:<port>`.

If you need more control over the configuration, consider registering one or more `PulsarAdminBuilderCustomizer` beans.

### Authentication

When accessing a Pulsar cluster that requires authentication, the admin client requires the same security configuration as the regular Pulsar client.
You can use the aforementioned authentication configuration by replacing `spring.pulsar.client.authentication` with `spring.pulsar.admin.authentication`.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | To create a topic on startup, add a bean of type `PulsarTopic`. If the topic already exists, the bean is ignored. |
questions: Given that the system auto-provisions a management client which by default targets a local HTTP development endpoint (on the conventional 8080 port), can have its connection target overridden via configuration, and requires the same security settings as the runtime messaging client (by replacing the runtime security property), what interoperability or operational constraint motivates reusing the runtime client's credentials for the management client, and how does that constraint interact with the design of an optional startup-time bean that attempts to create a named messaging destination but is simply ignored when the destination already exists?

content:
| `PulsarConnectionDetails` | Containers of type `PulsarContainer` |
| `R2dbcConnectionDetails` | Containers of type `ClickHouseContainer`, `MariaDBContainer`, `MSSQLServerContainer`, `MySQLContainer`, OracleContainer (free), OracleContainer (XE) or `PostgreSQLContainer` |
| `RabbitConnectionDetails` | Containers of type `RabbitMQContainer` |
| `DataRedisConnectionDetails` | Containers of type `RedisContainer` or `RedisStackContainer`, or containers named "redis", "redis/redis-stack" or "redis/redis-stack-server" |
| `ZipkinConnectionDetails` | Containers named "openzipkin/zipkin" |

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | By default all applicable connection details beans will be created for a given `Container`. For example, a `PostgreSQLContainer` will create both `JdbcConnectionDetails` and `R2dbcConnectionDetails`.  If you want to create only a subset of the applicable types, you can use the `type` attribute of `@ServiceConnection`. |
questions: Given that many different service images can satisfy the same connectivity abstraction while a single image will, by default, produce multiple connectivity-description beans, what practical interoperability or lifecycle concern would compel you to restrict which descriptions are emitted for that image, and which configurable selector on the declarative service-linking mechanism would you adjust to ensure only the desired subset is instantiated?

If a configuration-binding component only traverses and assigns values to an object supplied as a positional parameter of a compact immutable type when that parameter is explicitly marked with a nested-property marker, while for conventional mutable class declarations it instead requires publicly exposed accessors and mutators to perform binding, what fundamental limitation in the binder's introspection-and-instantiation strategy explains why both the explicit nested marker for parameter-based declarations and the public-access requirement for class-based declarations are necessary?

content:
## `@Transactional` Support

By default, `RedisTemplate` does not participate in managed Spring transactions.
If you want `RedisTemplate` to make use of Redis transaction when using `@Transactional` or `TransactionTemplate`, you need to be explicitly enable transaction support for each `RedisTemplate` by setting `setEnableTransactionSupport(true)`.
Enabling transaction support binds `RedisConnection` to the current transaction backed by a `ThreadLocal`.
If the transaction finishes without errors, the Redis transaction gets commited with `EXEC`, otherwise rolled back with `DISCARD`.
Redis transactions are batch-oriented.
Commands issued during an ongoing transaction are queued and only applied when committing the transaction.

Spring Data Redis distinguishes between read-only and write commands in an ongoing transaction.
Read-only commands, such as `KEYS`, are piped to a fresh (non-thread-bound) `RedisConnection` to allow reads.
questions: Considering that enabling transactional participation for the in-memory datastore client ties its network session to the live transactional context via thread-specific storage and causes issued commands to be buffered until the context is finalized, what inherent limitation of this binding-and-buffering approach compels the framework to route non-mutating read requests through a fresh, non-bound session?

content:
# Null Handling of Repository Methods

Repository CRUD methods that return an individual aggregate instances can use `Optional` to indicate the potential absence of a value.
Besides that, Spring Data supports returning the following wrapper types on query methods:

* `com.google.common.base.Optional`
* `scala.Option`
* `io.vavr.control.Option`

Alternatively, query methods can choose not to use a wrapper type at all.
The absence of a query result is then indicated by returning `null`.
Repository methods returning collections, collection alternatives, wrappers, and streams are guaranteed never to return `null` but rather the corresponding empty representation.
See “Repository query return types” for details.
questions: In a persistence API where single-entity lookups may either wrap a missing result in a purpose-built presence-indicating container (and the API even accepts several interchangeable flavors of such containers) or, alternatively, return a no-value sentinel when callers decline a wrapper, while multi-entity retrievals are guaranteed to yield an empty multi-item structure rather than that sentinel, what architectural constraints and client-side hazards motivate adopting this mixed convention, and how should client code alter its error-handling, composition, and interoperability strategies to remain correct and resilient across both single- and multi-entity call patterns?

Considering a framework that supplies a pre-wired constructor-helper to produce HTTP-calling client objects but intentionally refrains from exposing a single shared client instance, what architectural constraint—given teams' need to preserve legacy call patterns, the frequent requirement to customize each client instance, and the constructor-helper’s role in wiring appropriate payload serializers and underlying transport providers—motivates that decision?

content:
## Hot Swapping

Since Spring Boot applications are plain Java applications, JVM hot-swapping should work out of the box.
JVM hot swapping is somewhat limited with the bytecode that it can replace.
For a more complete solution, JRebel can be used.

The `spring-boot-devtools` module also includes support for quick application restarts.
See the Hot Swapping section in “How-to Guides” for details.
questions: Given that the application executes on a general managed runtime which permits only limited in-memory replacement of compiled artifacts, and that the project supplies a developer convenience module for rapid process restarts while a commercial live-editing agent offers broader change coverage, which inherent constraint of the compiled-artifact format limits what can be substituted at runtime and thus compels reliance on restart-based workflows or the paid agent?

content:
# JMS

The `ConnectionFactory` interface provides a standard method of creating a `Connection` for interacting with a JMS broker.
Although Spring needs a `ConnectionFactory` to work with JMS, you generally need not use it directly yourself and can instead rely on higher level messaging abstractions.
(See the relevant section of the Spring Framework reference documentation for details.)
Spring Boot also auto-configures the necessary infrastructure to send and receive messages.
questions: What specific limitations or developer-facing risks associated with directly managing the low-level provider that instantiates messaging connections—such as lifecycle complexity, configuration burden, or unsafe resource handling—motivated the framework designers to keep that provider under the hood, favor higher-level messaging APIs, and enable automatic infrastructure provisioning by default?

content:
# Recording HTTP Exchanges

You can enable recording of HTTP exchanges by providing a bean of type `HttpExchangeRepository` in your application’s configuration.
For convenience, Spring Boot offers `InMemoryHttpExchangeRepository`, which, by default, stores the last 100 request-response exchanges.
`InMemoryHttpExchangeRepository` is limited compared to tracing solutions, and we recommend using it only for development environments.
For production environments, we recommend using a production-ready tracing or observability solution, such as Zipkin or OpenTelemetry.
Alternatively, you can create your own `HttpExchangeRepository`.

You can use the `httpexchanges` endpoint to obtain information about the request-response exchanges that are stored in the `HttpExchangeRepository`.
questions: Which operational shortcomings would surface if an application treated its built-in, in‑process capture of recent request–response interactions (which only keeps roughly the last one hundred records and exposes them via a retrieval path) as a long‑term audit mechanism, and how do those shortcomings justify preferring a production‑grade observability backend or a custom persistent capture implementation instead?

If a persistence framework lets you substitute verbose implementation identifiers in stored records with concise per-entity labels via entity metadata, yet also exposes a conversion-layer mapping component that can be subclassed and registered to provide more advanced mappings, what limitations or operational constraints of the per-entity labeling approach would drive architects to implement and hook a centralized custom mapping component into the conversion layer instead of relying solely on the per-entity labels?

content:
For these reasons, lazy initialization is not enabled by default and it is recommended that fine-tuning of the JVM’s heap size is done before enabling lazy initialization.

Lazy initialization can be enabled programmatically using the `lazyInitialization` method on `SpringApplicationBuilder` or the `setLazyInitialization` method on `SpringApplication`.
Alternatively, it can be enabled using the `spring.main.lazy-initialization` property as shown in the following example:

* Properties
* YAML

```
spring.main.lazy-initialization=true
```

```
spring:
  main:
    lazy-initialization: true
```

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | If you want to disable lazy initialization for certain beans while using lazy initialization for the rest of the application, you can explicitly set their lazy attribute to false using the `@Lazy(false)` annotation. |
questions: Given that deferred component instantiation is turned off by default and operators are advised to adjust the Java runtime heap before enabling it, and that the behavior can be activated either via the application builder API or a configuration key while individual components may be opted out with a per-component annotation, what specific runtime resource limitation and resulting startup behaviour justify leaving deferred startup disabled by default, and how do the two activation paths and the per-component opt-out change the operator’s mitigation strategy?

If an application-level settings object that has been marked to undergo constraint-based checks declares a required network-endpoint field (whose type represents an IP/address) yet the binding phase still permits a null value at startup, which missing runtime library on the classpath explains that failure and why does the configuration-binding process depend on that library to enforce the declared requirement?

content:
For example, to map `404` to a static HTML file, your directory structure would be as follows:

```
src/
 +- main/
     +- java/
     |   + <source code>
     +- resources/
         +- public/
             +- error/
             |   +- 404.html
             +- <other public assets>
```

To map all `5xx` errors by using a Mustache template, your directory structure would be as follows:

```
src/
 +- main/
     +- java/
     |   + <source code>
     +- resources/
         +- templates/
             +- error/
             |   +- 5xx.mustache
             +- <other templates>
```

### Web Filters

Spring WebFlux provides a `WebFilter` interface that can be implemented to filter HTTP request-response exchanges.
`WebFilter` beans found in the application context will be automatically used to filter each exchange.

Where the order of the filters is important they can implement `Ordered` or be annotated with `@Order`.
Spring Boot auto-configuration may configure web filters for you.
questions: Given an application where status-specific response pages can be provided either as static files or as files processed by a view-templating engine, and where request–response interception components are automatically discovered and can indicate their execution precedence, what underlying constraints and failure modes compel developers to (a) place static versus templated view assets under particular application resource lookup subpaths and (b) explicitly set interception precedence—explain how these placement and precedence requirements interact to make those architectural decisions necessary?

Given an application framework that auto-provisions client implementations and exposes both a connection-factory abstraction and high-level template components for injection to interact with a remote in-memory key–value service, what practical limitation or trade-off in choosing a single default client for a general-purpose dependency bundle would nonetheless compel maintainers to publish a distinct dependency bundle focused solely on non-blocking interaction, and how does the choice to expose injectable factory/template beans factor into that decision?

This will trigger the import of a `dev.properties` file in current directory (if such a file exists).
Values from the imported `dev.properties` will take precedence over the file that triggered the import.
In the above example, the `dev.properties` could redefine `spring.application.name` to a different value.

An import will only be imported once no matter how many times it is declared.

#### Using “Fixed” and “Import Relative” Locations

Imports may be specified as *fixed* or *import relative* locations.
A fixed location always resolves to the same underlying resource, regardless of where the `spring.config.import` property is declared.
An import relative location resolves relative to the file that declares the `spring.config.import` property.

A location starting with a forward slash (`/`) or a URL style prefix (`file:`, `classpath:`, etc.) is considered fixed.
All other locations are considered import relative.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
questions: Given a configuration framework where a primary descriptor can reference an auxiliary descriptor that, when pulled in, supersedes the primary’s settings; references are either resolved as fixed addresses that always target the same resource regardless of where they are declared, or as addresses interpreted with respect to the location of the referencing descriptor, and each auxiliary is applied only once even if referenced repeatedly—explain why a fixed-address auxiliary will end up superseding a value defined in another descriptor, and how the apply-once behavior combined with location-relative resolution can cause subsequent re-references to that same auxiliary to have no additional effect.

Given that a client implementation must be declared in the project's build manifest and that the default, no-argument connection-supplying component can be replaced by one constructed from an object carrying an explicit network endpoint and authentication secrets, what deployment requirements and runtime limitations would motivate making both of those changes when promoting an application from development to production?

|  | You can use `@Qualifier("groupname")` if you need to register custom `StatusAggregator` or `HttpCodeStatusMapper` beans for use with the group. |

A health group can also include/exclude a `CompositeHealthContributor`.
You can also include/exclude only a certain component of a `CompositeHealthContributor`.
This can be done using the fully qualified name of the component as follows:

```
management.endpoint.health.group.custom.include="test/primary"
management.endpoint.health.group.custom.exclude="test/primary/b"
```

In the example above, the `custom` group will include the `HealthContributor` with the name `primary` which is a component of the composite `test`.
Here, `primary` itself is a composite and the `HealthContributor` with the name `b` will be excluded from the `custom` group.

Health groups can be made available at an additional path on either the main or management port.
questions: Given a system that assembles collections of runtime status checks and permits addressing a nested member of an aggregated checker by its path-like identifier, while also allowing such a collection to be exposed under an alternate endpoint on either the public service interface or the administrative interface, what practical naming or visibility issues would drive the designers to provide both of those capabilities?
